right.reveal: You leave the betting apps largely alone. The palms stay greased and the trade flourishes, and the young keep gambling their families' money into accounts somewhere overseas.

speaker: Forensic Services Director
prompt: The police science lab is too busy, so innocent people sit in jail while waiting for proof. You can spend money to build more science labs.

left.label: Spend money to build more science labs.
left.tooltip: Justice without science is justice by guesswork.
left.reveal: You fund new labs, equipment, and forensic scientists. The backlog shrinks over years, evidence starts reaching trials in time, and the reliance on beaten confessions slowly gives way to proof.

right.label: Leave the lab busy and let people wait in jail.
right.tooltip: Labs are a luxury; confessions are free.
right.reveal: You leave the single lab to drown in its backlog. The budget is spared, and the courts keep convicting on confession and guesswork while the science gathers dust in an evidence locker.

speaker: Police Procurement Chief
prompt: A seller wants to give you big, scary weapons like water cannons to use on angry crowds.

left.label: Buy the big, scary weapons.
left.tooltip: A well-equipped force controls a crowd without a massacre.
left.reveal: You acquire the arsenal. Crowd control gets a notch less lethal in theory, and in untrained hands the rubber bullets and gas still maim and kill, just with deniability.

right.label: Buy safer tools and teach the police to be careful.
right.tooltip: A weapon is only as humane as the hand that wields it.
right.reveal: You buy a modest kit and invest in restraint and training. The arsenal is smaller, but the officers learn to use it as a last resort, and crowd policing grows genuinely less deadly.

speaker: Women's Crime Officer
prompt: A young wife was hurt by her powerful new family, and they are paying the police to hide the crime.

left.label: Fight hard to punish the powerful family.
left.tooltip: A bride burned for money will have her day in court.
left.reveal: You protect the witnesses and push the case against the powerful in-laws. They lean on every connection and lose, and for once a dowry murder ends in a verdict instead of a settlement.

right.label: Let the powerful family get away with it.
right.tooltip: The in-laws have friends I would rather keep.
right.reveal: You let the case dissolve in bought witnesses and lost files. The powerful in-laws walk, the dead bride joins the uncounted, and every dowry-taker learns the price of a daughter-in-law's life.

speaker: Telecom Security Director
prompt: People are using secret phone tools to read news you banned. You can block the secret tools too, but people will be mad.

left.label: Block the secret tools and track the people using them.
left.tooltip: A wall with a tunnel under it is no wall.
left.reveal: You move to block VPNs and flag their users. The information bubble tightens toward North-Korean completeness, the tech-savvy find new tunnels anyway, and the censorship draws global scorn.

right.label: Accept that you can't block everything on the internet.
right.tooltip: A government at war with VPNs is a government afraid of news.
right.reveal: You stop chasing VPNs. The blocks stay porous and the banned sites reachable, but you are spared the futile, reputation-shredding crusade against the open internet.

speaker: Social Welfare Director
prompt: Many poor children live on the city streets and are in danger. You can build safe homes for them, or just keep chasing them away.

left.label: Build safe homes to help the street children.
left.tooltip: A child on the street is a future thrown away.
left.reveal: You build shelters, schooling, and family-tracing for street children. The cost is real and the work slow, but children are pulled off the streets into something like a childhood.

right.label: Just keep chasing them off the streets.
right.tooltip: A shelter network is a budget I do not have.
right.reveal: You keep the cheap round-up drives that scoop children up and dump them elsewhere. The streets look briefly clearer, and the children drift back to the glue, the gangs, and the traffickers.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: Bad people are using fake pictures online to bully and scare girls.

left.label: Build a special, safe police team to help the girls.
left.tooltip: A girl shamed to death by a screen deserved a state that fought for her.
left.reveal: You stand up a confidential unit that pursues the blackmailers and protects the victims. The cultural pushback is real, but women begin to report without being destroyed for it.

right.label: Leave it to the regular police.
right.tooltip: These are private matters, not police priorities.
right.reveal: You leave image-abuse to the ordinary stations that blame and expose the victims. The blackmailers operate freely, and the suicides keep coming, each one a girl the state would not protect.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: There are too many giant parties happening at once, and crowds might get squished and hurt.

left.label: Make strict rules to keep the crowds safe.
left.tooltip: A joyous crowd can become a crush in a heartbeat.
left.reveal: You enforce capacity caps, exits, and crowd management. The organisers grumble at the cost and the public at the fences, but the weekend's celebrations end without a stampede to bury.

right.label: Let the parties happen without safety rules.
right.tooltip: Do not smother a celebration in barriers.
right.reveal: You keep the events open and lightly managed. The crowds flow free and vast and joyous, and the absence of a plan means one surge from turning a festival into a funeral.

speaker: Rural Justice Officer
prompt: In faraway villages, angry mobs are hurting poor women by calling them witches.

left.label: Send police to punish the mobs and teach them it is wrong.
left.tooltip: A woman killed as a witch is a murder, not a custom.
left.reveal: You prosecute the mob leaders and run awareness drives with the rural clergy and schools. Change is slow against deep superstition, but the killings draw consequences and the accused gain protectors.

right.label: Ignore it and say it's just their old tradition.
right.tooltip: I cannot police every village's superstitions.
right.reveal: You leave the matter to the locals. The 'healers' keep naming witches, the mobs keep their impunity, and poor women keep being beaten and burned for crimes that do not exist.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: Bad workers are secretly selling your country's official ID cards to strangers and criminals.

left.label: Catch the bad workers and stop the fake cards.
left.tooltip: A citizenship sold is a nation cheapened.
left.reveal: You audit the database, cancel the fraudulent IDs, and jail the officials who sold them. The system's integrity is restored at the cost of a noisy scandal and some genuinely caught-up innocents.

right.label: Hide the problem so you don't look silly.
right.tooltip: A public purge advertises how porous we are.
right.reveal: You handle it discreetly, cancelling a few cards and hushing the rest. The scandal stays small, and the corrupt officials and the trade in genuine IDs to non-citizens carry quietly on.

speaker: Registrar General
prompt: People are changing papers to let little girls get married illegally. You can use computers to stop this.

left.label: Use computers to prove real ages and protect girls.
left.tooltip: A registrar who forges a child's age is an accomplice to her ruin.
left.reveal: You digitise registration with hard age verification. The registrars' guild howls at the lost fees, but child marriages lose their paper cover and the registry stops abetting them.

right.label: Let the people keep changing the papers.
right.tooltip: A connected guild is not lightly disturbed.
right.reveal: You leave the marriage registry as it is. The registrars keep their fees and their forgeries, and the falsified ages keep legalising the marriage of children across the country.

speaker: Narcotics Control Chief
prompt: Hundreds of thousands of people are sick from bad drugs. You can build nice hospitals to help them, or keep throwing them in jail.

left.label: Build nice hospitals to help sick drug users.
left.tooltip: An addict is a patient, not a body for the morgue.
left.reveal: You build accessible, humane rehabilitation as the spine of drug policy. The tough-on-drugs crowd scoffs, but families get their children back from addiction instead of from the river.

right.label: Keep throwing them in jail.
right.tooltip: Treatment coddles the weak; fear deters the rest.
right.reveal: You keep drug policy punitive. The squalid centres stay squalid and the addicted cycle through cells and crossfire, and the addiction the crackdowns were meant to crush keeps spreading.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: Bad gangs with guns are digging up the rivers to sell the sand, and paying police to stay quiet.

left.label: Fight the gangs and stop them from ruining the river.
left.tooltip: A river ruled by armed thieves is a writ of failure.
left.reveal: You move hard against the sand mafias and the officers and politicians shielding them. It is dangerous — they fight and kill — but the riverbanks are wrested back and the racket's cover is torn off.

right.label: Let the gangs keep digging up the sand.
right.tooltip: The men who run the sand also run the wards.
right.reveal: You leave the sand mafias to their riverbanks. The peace holds and the cover stays, and the illegal dredging guts the rivers while anyone who objects risks a body in the current.

speaker: Anti-Encroachment Officer
prompt: You are knocking down houses built in the wrong places. But you only knock down poor people's houses, not rich people's big buildings.

left.label: Knock down the rich people's illegal buildings too.
left.tooltip: A bulldozer that only finds the poor is a class weapon.
left.reveal: You redirect the drive at the powerful's illegal grabs — the riverside mansions, the encroaching towers. The connected scream and litigate, but the law's bulldozer finally faces upward.

right.label: Only knock down the poor people's houses.
right.tooltip: The big buildings have lawyers; the slums do not.
right.reveal: You keep the drive pointed at the slums and hawkers. The optics of progress are cheap and easy, the powerful's grand encroachments stand serene, and the poor learn whose law this is.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: The electricity keeps turning off in the summer, and angry people are breaking things in the street.

left.label: Use the police to force the angry people away.
left.tooltip: A mob at the substation is a mob, whatever its grievance.
left.reveal: You break up the protests with force. The streets clear and the substations are guarded, but beating people over power cuts they did not cause only stokes the anger in the dark.

right.label: Show you are sorry and focus on fixing the electricity.
right.tooltip: You cannot baton a country into cool patience.
right.reveal: You pull police back, publicise load-shedding equally for the elite too, and rush repairs. The protests cool as people see the burden shared, and the anger drains toward patience.

speaker: Aviation Security Chief
prompt: Airport workers are helping bad guys sneak gold and drugs through the airport.

left.label: Fire the bad workers and make the airport safe.
left.tooltip: An airport for sale is a border for sale.
left.reveal: You overhaul screening and purge the complicit staff. Foreign airlines relax, the smuggling chokes, and the ground-staff syndicates and their patrons lose a goldmine and turn on you.

right.label: Let the airport workers keep sneaking things.
right.tooltip: A purge would ground half the staff and the flights.
right.reveal: You leave the airport's shadow economy intact. The flights run smoothly and the rackets thrive, and the gold and drugs keep flowing while foreign carriers quietly downgrade their security rating of you.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The jails are way too full. You can teach prisoners good skills to use when they get out, or let bad guards force them to work for free.

left.label: Teach the prisoners good skills to help them.
left.tooltip: A prisoner taught a trade returns a citizen.
left.reveal: You build a real rehabilitation-through-work programme with wages and training. The reform is costly and the corrupt officers lose their private labour racket, but inmates leave with a skill instead of a grudge.

right.label: Let the guards force them to work for free.
right.tooltip: A busy prisoner is a quiet prisoner.
right.reveal: You leave prison labour to the officers' private schemes. Some inmates are quietly exploited for profit, the overcrowding and idleness fester, and the jails keep manufacturing hardened, embittered men.

speaker: Returnee Affairs Officer
prompt: Many workers lost their jobs in other countries and came back with no money.

left.label: Spend money to help them find new jobs at home.
left.tooltip: A returnee abandoned is a grievance recruited.
left.reveal: You build reintegration support — credit, retraining, counselling — for the returnees. The cost is real, but a destabilising mass of broke, angry men is turned toward livelihoods instead of unrest.

right.label: Let them figure it out on their own.
right.tooltip: The state did not send them; it cannot catch them all.
right.reveal: You leave the returnees to their own devices. The budget is spared, and a vast cohort of jobless, indebted, traumatised men sinks into despair, crime, and the recruiters' waiting arms.

speaker: Diplomatic Security Chief
prompt: Spies say bad guys want to attack a building where people from other countries work.

left.label: Put lots of loud, visible police everywhere to scare bad guys.
left.tooltip: A guarded embassy is a standing embassy.
left.reveal: You ring the enclave with heavy security. The diplomats are reassured and the plot likely deterred, but the fortress optics broadcast a country under siege and unsettle the very investors you court.

right.label: Use secret spies to catch the bad guys quietly.
right.tooltip: A wall of guns advertises the fear it answers.
right.reveal: You opt for discreet, intelligence-driven security and roll up the plotters quietly. It is a gamble on your spies being right, but the enclave stays calm and the country looks composed.

speaker: Aviation Security Chief
prompt: You can make a list of bad people who aren't allowed to fly on airplanes. But you might be tempted to put reporters and enemies on the list too.

left.label: Make a big list and include your enemies.
left.tooltip: Better a hundred grounded than one bomber boarding.
left.reveal: You build a broad watchlist with loose criteria. A few genuine threats are grounded, and so are journalists and activists who find themselves quietly barred from boarding under a label they cannot contest.

right.label: Make a strict list only for real, dangerous bad guys.
right.tooltip: A watchlist with no judge is a blacklist with wings.
right.reveal: You build a narrow list with judicial review and appeal. It is slower and the security hawks complain, but it stops the terror label from becoming a travel ban on dissent.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: The police want a rule to collect the names of every kid who plays video games at the store, just to spy on them.

left.label: Make the stores collect everyone's names.
left.tooltip: A screen unwatched is a plot unseen.
left.reveal: You require the logs and the software. A few real cases surface, and every student who plays a game or reads the news in a cybercafe now does so under the state's recorded eye.

right.label: Let the kids play games in private.
right.tooltip: A boy at a screen is not a suspect.
right.reveal: You drop the surveillance mandate. You forgo a thin investigative edge, but the cybercafes stay places of private curiosity rather than logged interrogation rooms.

speaker: Women's Crime Officer
prompt: A rich, powerful boy threw chemicals at a girl's face. His family is paying the police to drop the case.

left.label: Fight the rich family and protect the girl.
left.tooltip: A face melted for a refusal will have its reckoning.
left.reveal: You protect the witnesses, fund the survivor's reconstruction, and push the case against the powerful family. They lean and lose, and an acid attacker faces the gallows the law prescribes.

right.label: Let the case quietly disappear.
right.tooltip: The boy's father is a friend I cannot lose.
right.reveal: You let the file dissolve. The disfigured woman gets neither justice nor care, the powerful family walks, and every spurned man with acid and connections takes note.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: People's family members are going missing, and the police refuse to even help search for them.

left.label: Make a real rule that police must help search for missing people.
left.tooltip: A family that cannot even file a report has been failed twice.
left.reveal: You mandate prompt reporting, a public registry, and real investigation. Genuine missing-persons cases get traction, and so does an uncomfortable record of those who vanished into the state's own hands.

right.label: Let the police keep ignoring the families.
right.tooltip: A registry of the missing is a registry of our sins.
right.reveal: You leave the practice untouched. The families keep being turned from the gates, the missing stay officially un-missing, and the disappearances leave no paper trail to haunt you.

speaker: Police Welfare Director
prompt: Your normal police officers are tired, poor, and live in dirty rooms. This makes them mean and want bribes.

left.label: Spend money to pay them better and give them nice rooms.
left.tooltip: A constable treated like dirt treats the public like dirt.
left.reveal: You fund better pay, housing, and humane hours. The cost is heavy and the public initially grudging, but morale lifts, and a force that is not desperate leans a little less on the baton and the bribe.

right.label: Leave the police poor and tired.
right.tooltip: The budget has worthier claims than a policeman's pillow.
right.reveal: You leave the constabulary in its squalor. The money is saved, and the overworked, underpaid force keeps taking its frustration out on the public and its shortfall out of their pockets.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: The world wants to know what happened to the people your secret police kidnapped.

left.label: Keep lying and say you don't know anything.
left.tooltip: An admission is a noose I tie for myself.
left.reveal: You maintain the blanket denial. The denials grow more threadbare as survivors and bodies surface, and the disappearances calcify into the defining crime of your rule.

right.label: Bring back the ones who are alive and tell the truth.
right.tooltip: A wound this deep can only close with the truth.
right.reveal: You quietly release the living and acknowledge the dead, families weeping at gates and gravesides. The security establishment panics at the precedent, but a monstrous chapter begins, at last, to close.

speaker: Hill Tracts Security Officer
prompt: Soldiers are being very mean to the native people in the hills, searching them for no reason.

left.label: Tell the soldiers to be nice and respectful.
left.tooltip: A people frisked daily learns to hate daily.
left.reveal: You enforce conduct rules, end the shakedowns, and treat hill travellers as citizens. The hard-liners in the brass grumble, but the daily humiliation eases and a grievance loses some of its heat.

right.label: Let the soldiers keep being mean.
right.tooltip: A loosened grip in the hills invites the gun.
right.reveal: You leave the checkpoints to their ways. The settlers pass and the indigenous are shaken down, and every humiliation at the barrier quietly recruits for the insurgency the hard hand was meant to deter.

speaker: Drug Administration Chief
prompt: Stores are selling fake medicine that doesn't work and hurts sick people.

left.label: Stop the fake medicine and check every pill.
left.tooltip: A fake pill is a slow bullet.
left.reveal: You smash the counterfeit ring and require track-and-trace on medicines. The connected distributors fight back, but the fake drugs recede from the pharmacy shelves and patients stop dying from chalk.

right.label: Let the stores keep selling whatever they want.
right.tooltip: A crackdown would disrupt the whole supply.
right.reveal: You leave the counterfeit trade largely alone. The pharmacies stay stocked and the racket profitable, and patients keep swallowing chalk antibiotics and fake cancer drugs and quietly dying of treatable things.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Secret police in normal clothes are grabbing people off the street without rules.

left.label: Force the police to wear uniforms and follow the rules.
left.tooltip: An arrest with no record is an abduction with a badge.
left.reveal: You mandate identification, paperwork, and immediate logging for every arrest. The agencies complain about lost speed and surprise, but the line between policing and disappearance is redrawn.

right.label: Let the secret police keep grabbing people.
right.tooltip: An officer slowed by paperwork is an officer outrun.
right.reveal: You leave the unmarked-car arrests unregulated. The agencies keep their speed and their shadows, and the families keep standing at police gates that deny the person was ever taken.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: Before big parties, your team forces store owners to give them "donations" of money.

left.label: Stop your team from forcing people to pay.
left.tooltip: A shopkeeper shaken down in my name curses my name.
left.reveal: You order the police to stop the collections and arrest the worst extortionists. The cadres feel betrayed and the local bosses grumble, but the traders breathe and the party's name stops being a threat.

right.label: Let your team keep taking the money.
right.tooltip: The foot-soldiers must eat between elections.
right.reveal: You leave the racket alone. The cadres feed and stay loyal, and every festival the shopkeepers and truckers pay a tax to the party's name while the police watch and wet their beaks.

speaker: Law Secretary
prompt: You can write a new law that says anyone who complains about you is a "terrorist."

left.label: Write the unfair law to lock up complainers.
left.tooltip: A wide net catches the threat before it forms.
left.reveal: You pass a sweeping definition that sweeps in dissent. Genuine plots and peaceful critics alike now risk the terror label, and the law becomes your most flexible cudgel and a global byword for overreach.

right.label: Make sure the law is only for real, dangerous terrorists.
right.tooltip: A law that calls a strike terror is a law against the people.
right.reveal: You keep the definition tight to real violence. The security hawks complain it ties their hands, but protest and dissent stay outside the terror net, and the law keeps its legitimacy.

speaker: Fire Investigation Officer
prompt: A rich builder probably started a fire to burn down poor people's houses so he can take their land.

left.label: Investigate the rich builder for starting the fire.
left.tooltip: A slum that burns whenever a developer wants it is no accident.
left.reveal: You launch a real investigation pointing at the land's would-be owner. The developer and his patrons push hard, but the residents see the state take their suspicion seriously for once.

right.label: Lie and say it was just an accidental fire.
right.tooltip: An arson case against a developer is a war I need not start.
right.reveal: You close it as a wiring accident. The developer gets his cleared land in due course, the displaced scatter, and the convenient slum fire joins a long line of convenient slum fires.

speaker: Public Order Officer
prompt: Small religious groups are having parties, but they are getting threats online. You can send extra police to protect them.

left.label: Send lots of police to protect their parties.
left.tooltip: A festival that needs guarding will be guarded.
left.reveal: You deploy real protection around the temples, monasteries, and churches. The hardliners mutter about appeasement, but the minorities celebrate in peace and see a state that counts them as its own.

right.label: Don't send extra police, to keep the big angry groups happy.
right.tooltip: I will not garrison every shrine and provoke the majority.
right.reveal: You provide token security. The hardliners are unprovoked and content, and the minorities celebrate under a shadow, one rumour away from the attack the thin protection cannot stop.

speaker: Camp Security Chief
prompt: You can put sharp fences and guard towers around the homeless camps like a giant prison to keep everyone inside.

left.label: Build the sharp fences around the camps.
left.tooltip: An unfenced camp leaks crime and militancy into my towns.
left.reveal: You wire in the camps. The host communities' fears ease and movement is controlled, and the UN and rights world condemn the caging of a stateless people who fled exactly this.

right.label: Manage the camps without turning them into a prison.
right.tooltip: A people who fled a cage should not find another.
right.reveal: You manage security with policing and registration rather than barbed wire. The host communities fret about the open edges, but you avoid building the prison-camp optics the agencies dread.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Police stations are scary and mean to poor people and women. You can spend money to make them friendly and helpful.

left.label: Make the police stations friendly and helpful.
left.tooltip: A thana the poor fear protects no one.
left.reveal: You rebuild the stations around service — women's desks, tracked complaints, accountable reception. The old culture resists, but ordinary people start to see the police as something other than a threat.

right.label: Leave the police stations scary and mean.
right.tooltip: A police station is meant to be feared.
right.reveal: You leave the stations intimidating and opaque. The money is saved, and the poor and women keep avoiding the very institution meant to protect them, suffering crimes rather than reporting them.

speaker: Intelligence Chief
prompt: Spies want to sneak into places where people pray to secretly listen to what they say.

left.label: Let the spies secretly listen to people praying.
left.tooltip: Radicalisation is preached before it is plotted.
left.reveal: You seed the mosques with informants. A few cells are caught early, and when the network is exposed — as it will be — the devout erupt at a state that spies on prayer itself.

right.label: Do not spy on people when they are praying.
right.tooltip: A spy in the mosque makes an enemy of the congregation.
right.reveal: You work through trusted community engagement rather than informants. The intelligence is thinner, but you do not pit the entire devout population against a state caught surveilling their worship.

speaker: Home Affairs Coordinator
prompt: An angry mob burned a neighborhood, and the police just watched.

left.label: Find out the truth openly and pay the victims for the damage.
left.tooltip: A pogrom the police watched is a pogrom the state owns.
left.reveal: You order an independent inquiry and full reparations. The force is rattled and a few officers fall, but the minority community sees accountability and the message lands that the police are not above the law.

right.label: Hide the truth to protect the police who watched.
right.tooltip: An independent inquiry indicts the whole force.
right.reveal: You keep the review in-house and it absolves the police. The compensation is token, the officers protected, and the minority neighbourhood learns that those who burned it and those who watched will answer to no one.

speaker: Cyber Crime Chief
prompt: The computer police want to spy on all students' internet posts to see if they are planning protests.

left.label: Let the police spy on the students' internet posts.
left.tooltip: A movement watched in the cradle is a movement controlled.
left.reveal: You set the cyber unit on the students' networks and pre-empt 'instigators'. A few flashpoints are defused, and a generation learns that its every online word is watched, breeding the resentment that fuels the next eruption.

right.label: Leave the students' internet posts alone.
right.tooltip: A government that surveils its students fears its future.
right.reveal: You decline to surveil the students. You lose an early-warning tool, but the young keep a space to argue and organise freely, and you are not the government that spied on its own schoolchildren.

speaker: Crime Investigation Chief
prompt: Rich criminals go free because they scare the people who saw the crime. You can spend money to protect the witnesses.

left.label: Create a safe program to protect the witnesses.
left.tooltip: A witness who can be reached is a verdict that can be bought.
left.reveal: You build genuine witness protection — relocation, security, anonymity. It is costly and hard to run, but suddenly cases against the powerful hold, and the era of the bought-off witness begins to end.

right.label: Let the rich criminals keep scaring the witnesses.
right.tooltip: A protection programme is a budget and a headache.
right.reveal: You decline the programme. The powerful keep buying and burying witnesses, the cases against them keep collapsing, and the brave who testify keep paying for it in blood or worse.

speaker: Border Guard Director
prompt: Your border guards shot poor, unarmed villagers for crossing the line to trade.

left.label: Make a strict rule that guards cannot shoot unarmed people.
left.tooltip: A guard who shoots a smuggler boy is no guard.
left.reveal: You impose strict non-lethal engagement and discipline the trigger-happy. The brass grumbles about a softer border, but the killings of unarmed villagers stop staining your own hands.

right.label: Let the guards keep shooting people to scare them.
right.tooltip: A hard border is kept with a hard hand.
right.reveal: You leave the lethal posture in place. The border looks fierce, and your guards keep killing unarmed villagers over cattle and rice, handing the moral high ground and the headlines to your critics.

speaker: District Police Superintendent
prompt: The police want to pay poor people to spy on their neighbors. This will make everyone afraid of each other.

left.label: Pay people to spy on their neighbors.
left.tooltip: Eyes in every alley miss nothing.
left.reveal: You weave informants through the slums. Crime intelligence improves, and so does the fear, as neighbours inform on neighbours and petty grudges become police cases and the poor learn to trust no one.

right.label: Trust the community instead of paying spies.
right.tooltip: A neighbourhood of informers is a neighbourhood at war with itself.
right.reveal: You build trust-based community policing rather than an informant web. The intelligence is less granular, but the slums keep their fragile social fabric and the police gain genuine cooperation.

speaker: Surveillance Technology Director
prompt: You can use computers to scan everyone's faces in a crowd to know exactly who went to a protest.

left.label: Scan the faces of everyone in the crowd.
left.tooltip: A face in a crowd should not be a face I cannot name.
left.reveal: You deploy live facial scanning at gatherings. Every protester, worshipper, and marcher is now identifiable and loggable, and the chilling knowledge of it thins the crowds that dare to assemble.

right.label: Ban the computers from scanning people's faces at protests.
right.tooltip: A right to assemble means a right to assemble unnamed.
right.reveal: You forbid scanning peaceful gatherings. You forgo a powerful identification tool, but the anonymity that lets citizens protest and worship without fear of a logged face survives.

speaker: Prisons Medical Officer
prompt: Prisoners are getting sick and dying because there are no doctors in the jails.

left.label: Spend money to bring doctors to the jails.
left.tooltip: A sentence is loss of liberty, not a death warrant.
left.reveal: You fund real medical care in the jails. There are no votes in it and the cost is real, but prisoners stop dying of treatable illness, and the state stops being a quiet executioner by neglect.

right.label: Let the prisoners stay sick without doctors.
right.tooltip: A convict's health is the lowest claim on the budget.
right.reveal: You leave the jails without real medicine. The money goes to constituencies that vote, and prisoners keep dying of curable things, their deaths logged as misfortune and forgotten.

speaker: Rural Police Chief
prompt: A rich bully is stealing a poor family's land, and he paid the local police to ignore it.

left.label: Stop the rich bully and protect the poor family.
left.tooltip: A poor man's deed is worth as much as a rich man's.
left.reveal: You send honest officers, protect the family, and move against the grabber and the bent local chief. The powerful man rages and pulls strings, but the family keeps its land and the law its meaning.

right.label: Ignore it and pretend it's none of your business.
right.tooltip: I cannot referee every village land quarrel.
right.reveal: You stay out and call it a civil matter. The powerful man's muscle prevails, the family is dispossessed, and the lesson spreads that land here belongs to whoever can hire the most fists.

speaker: Hospital Liaison Officer
prompt: The police are going into hospitals and dragging hurt protesters out of their beds to arrest them.

left.label: Make hospitals safe places where police cannot arrest sick people.
left.tooltip: A wounded man on a gurney is a patient, not a target.
left.reveal: You order hospitals off-limits to arrests and protect treating doctors. The security forces fume at the sanctuary, but the wounded are treated and the state is not seen dragging the bleeding from their beds.

right.label: Let the police keep taking hurt people from hospitals.
right.tooltip: A hospital bed is no refuge from the law.
right.reveal: You let police seize the injured from the wards and lean on the doctors. The wounded learn that to seek treatment is to be arrested, and the images of patients hauled from gurneys travel the world.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: A man who argued with your powerful friend was kidnapped by secret police.

left.label: Investigate honestly and find the man, even if your friend is guilty.
left.tooltip: A disappearance I refuse to solve is one I own.
left.reveal: You order a real investigation, and it points toward your own. The minister and the agencies close ranks against you, but the businessman's fate is uncovered and the rot, for once, is named.

right.label: Secretly close the case to protect your friend.
right.tooltip: Some threads are better not pulled.
right.reveal: You bury the investigation. The minister is protected and the agencies reassured, and another man simply ceases to exist, his family left to a silence the state enforces.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: A reporter found out about the kidnapped man and is going to tell the world.

left.label: Hurt the reporter to stop the story.
left.tooltip: What is never reported never happened.
left.reveal: You spike the story and threaten the journalist into silence. The suppression itself leaks, and now you own both the disappearance and the cover-up of the cover-up, a scandal compounding on itself.

right.label: Admit it happened and blame one bad helper.
right.tooltip: A wound I lance heals; one I hide festers.
right.reveal: You concede the broad truth, offer a scapegoat and an inquiry, and free what can be freed. The base is unsettled and the agencies wary, but you blunt the story by meeting it rather than fleeing it.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. Students are blocking the roads peacefully to protest an unfair rule. The police chief wants to force them to leave right now.

left.label: Tell the police to force the students off the roads.
left.tooltip: A crowd unanswered only multiplies.
left.reveal: The batons and gas clear the roads for a day. The footage of bloodied students travels the world and triples the next crowd.

right.label: Tell the police to wait and try talking to the students.
right.tooltip: You cannot tear-gas a generation into loving you.
right.reveal: You meet the student leaders. The cantonment frets that you flinched; the campuses, astonished, lower the temperature.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. A video shows a police officer shooting an unarmed student. Everyone is very angry. Your strict friends want you to lock everyone inside and turn off the internet.

left.label: Lock everyone inside and turn off the internet.
left.tooltip: Silence the signal, scatter the crowd.
left.reveal: The blackout buys an eerie quiet and global condemnation. Businesses lose crores a day and the anger compounds in the dark.

right.label: Keep the internet on and investigate the shooting.
right.tooltip: A government that hides has already lost.
right.reveal: You name an inquiry and let the country watch. It is a gamble on transparency; the movement, given a hearing, begins to fracture between moderates and absolutists.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. The protesters want you to fire the leaders who ordered the police to hurt people. The army says they won't help you hurt students anymore.

left.label: Fire the leaders who ordered the bad things.
left.tooltip: Better to lose a wing than the whole bird.
left.reveal: You dismiss the ministers blamed for the deaths. The crowd claims a scalp and pauses; your inner circle learns no one is safe.

right.label: Protect your leaders and don't give up.
right.tooltip: I will not feed my own people to a mob.
right.reveal: You refuse to bend and rally the loyal core. But with the army unwilling to shoot, your authority now rests on a crowd's patience.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. A helper brings proof that your secret police kidnapped people and hid them in secret jails. Some are still alive.

left.label: Hide the proof and do nothing.
left.tooltip: The battalion is the spine of internal order.
left.reveal: The file vanishes and the cells stay full. The disappeared remain disappeared, and the rot spreads through the ranks that learn it is permitted.

right.label: Free the hidden people and investigate.
right.tooltip: A state that disappears its own has already fallen.
right.reveal: Survivors come home to weeping families and cameras. The battalion's commanders feel the betrayal; the world cautiously applauds.

speaker: Rights Commission Chair
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. The police are scared they will get in trouble for the bad things they did. They want you to promise to protect them, or they might stop listening to you.

left.label: Promise to protect them so they stay loyal.
left.tooltip: Loyalty now outweighs justice later.
left.reveal: The officers stand down, reassured. Impunity is enshrined, and the families of the disappeared learn there will be no reckoning.

right.label: Refuse to protect them and fix the police force instead.
right.tooltip: I will not be blackmailed by my own guns.
right.reveal: You retire the worst commanders and split the battalion's powers. It is dangerous — a wounded force is unpredictable — but the precedent of accountability holds.

speaker: ICT Enforcement Director
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. People are in jail just for drawing funny pictures or writing jokes about your family online. The police want to arrest more people.

left.label: Arrest more people to scare everyone into being quiet.
left.tooltip: Mockery unchecked becomes contempt.
left.reveal: The arrests chill the newsrooms into caution. Editors self-censor, and 'press freedom' becomes the West's favourite stick to beat you with.

right.label: Let the jokers out of jail and change the harsh rule.
right.tooltip: A government afraid of cartoons is afraid of itself.
right.reveal: The journalists walk free to grateful coverage. Your thin-skinned loyalists fume; your standing with the open world recovers.

speaker: District Magistrate, riot zone
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. An untrue rumor made angry people start breaking into homes and temples. The local police are just watching and not helping.

left.label: Send good police fast to arrest the bad people breaking things.
left.tooltip: A pogrom on my watch is my disgrace.
left.reveal: Troops restore order overnight and the ringleaders are jailed. The hardline preachers who fanned it turn their sermons against you.

right.label: Send a polite message but don't take sides.
right.tooltip: Do not inflame the majority before an election.
right.reveal: You stay above it and let it burn out. The minorities learn their safety is negotiable; the cynics call it electoral arithmetic.

speaker: Cyber Intelligence Vendor
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. A salesman offers you a secret tool to hack into anyone's phone and read their messages.

left.label: Buy the secret hacking tool and use it on anyone.
left.tooltip: Knowledge of every plot is worth the cost.
left.reveal: You gain a god's-eye view of your enemies — and your allies. When its existence leaks, as such things do, the scandal is radioactive.

right.label: Say no, or only use it when a judge says it's okay.
right.tooltip: A tool with no leash is a leash on me.
right.reveal: You either pass or wrap it in court oversight. You lose some omniscience; you keep a state that can still call itself lawful.

speaker: Reform-minded Police Superintendent
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. A good young police boss says a bad officer hurt a poor man until he died, then lied about it.

left.label: Tell the good boss to sign the lie and stay quiet.
left.tooltip: One hawker is not worth a war in the ranks.
left.reveal: The report is falsified and the SP, complicit now, climbs. Every constable learns the lock-up has no witnesses that matter.

right.label: Help the good boss and arrest the bad officer.
right.tooltip: A force that tortures the poor protects no one.
right.reveal: You make the honest officer a symbol and the OC an example. The old guard closes ranks against you; the public, briefly, believes in the police.

speaker: Camp Security Commander
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Bad gangs are doing illegal things in the homeless camps. The guard wants to use strong force to clean it up, but that will be very violent.

left.label: Let the guard use strong force to clean the camps.
left.tooltip: A lawless camp threatens the whole coast.
left.reveal: The gangs are crushed and the camp goes quiet. Civilian casualties and footage of the sweep hand your critics a year of ammunition.

right.label: Ask safe helpers to build a better, kinder camp police.
right.tooltip: Order earned beats order imposed.
right.reveal: You stand up a joint, accountable force. It is slow and imperfect, but the worst gangs lose their grip without a massacre.

speaker: Special Branch Director
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. An election is coming. A spy boss wants to arrest your enemies now for fake reasons so they can't campaign against you.

left.label: Say yes and arrest your enemies.
left.tooltip: A contest you control is a contest you win.
left.reveal: Thousands of organisers are jailed on old cases. The opposition cries foul to a sympathetic world; the result is yours and tainted.

right.label: Say no and let them campaign fairly.
right.tooltip: A win that needs empty jails is a loss in disguise.
right.reveal: You let the opposition march. The contest is real and dangerous; if you win, you win something worth holding.

speaker: Anti-Narcotics Director
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Bad drugs are everywhere. The police want to fight a harsh war and might accidentally kill people they think are selling drugs.

left.label: Let the police fight the harsh, dangerous war.
left.tooltip: Burn the trade out, root and branch.
left.reveal: The headlines blaze with seizures. The 'crossfire' bodies pile up, many of them poor and unproven, and the rights world recoils.

right.label: Treat drug users like sick people and only chase the big bosses.
right.tooltip: You cannot shoot your way out of a chemical.
right.reveal: You build clinics and chase the financiers instead. It is slow and unglamorous; the slums, in time, breathe easier.

speaker: Road Safety Movement
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. A bad bus driver ran over two students. Now all the kids are in the streets demanding safer roads.

left.label: Agree with the kids and make strict rules for safe roads.
left.tooltip: The children are policing what we would not.
left.reveal: You meet their demands and jail the worst operators. The transport-owner lobby, thick with your men, is enraged.

right.label: Force the kids off the streets and say they are lying.
right.tooltip: Schoolchildren do not run my roads.
right.reveal: Your cadres and police break the movement. The images of beaten students travel the world and stain the year.

speaker: District Police Super
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. A fake story on phones about people stealing children is making angry mobs beat up innocent strangers.

left.label: Turn off the phones and internet to stop the rumor.
left.tooltip: Silence the lie, save the lives.
left.reveal: The lynchings ebb as connectivity drops and patrols rise. Businesses and rights groups protest the heavy throttle.

right.label: Keep the internet on and tell everyone the truth loudly.
right.tooltip: You fight a rumour with the truth, not a blackout.
right.reveal: You blanket the airwaves with the facts and arrest ringleaders. Slower to bite, but the open net is preserved.

speaker: Hill Tracts Commander
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. People in the hills are fighting because an old peace promise is falling apart. The army wants to go in strong.

left.label: Send the army in strong to stop the fighting.
left.tooltip: Restore the writ of the state in the hills.
left.reveal: The faction scatters under pressure. Indigenous villages report abuses, settlers push deeper, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Fix the peace promise and protect the hill people.
right.tooltip: The hills were promised autonomy; honour it.
right.reveal: You restart talks and halt the land grab. The settler lobby and some generals seethe; the hills, warily, quiet.

speaker: Women's Rights Commission
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. A woman was hurt very badly. People are marching and demanding that the bad guys be put to death.

left.label: Make a law to put them to death to make the crowd happy.
left.tooltip: Give the street the sentence it demands.
left.reveal: The crowd is sated by the gallows clause. Conviction rates barely move; the deeper rot in policing and courts is untouched.

right.label: Build faster, better courts to protect women properly.
right.tooltip: A noose is not a justice system.
right.reveal: You fund the slow machinery of real protection. Less cathartic, but conviction and reporting finally begin to climb.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. Bad men in your country are tricking poor people onto dangerous boats to go far away. The bad men pay the police to look the other way.

left.label: Stop the bad men and the dirty police.
left.tooltip: We bury our youth in the Mediterranean.
left.reveal: You jail brokers and the officers on their payroll. The trade fragments; the bought policemen and their patrons turn on you.

right.label: Ignore it because those poor people might send money back later.
right.tooltip: Desperate men will go regardless.
right.reveal: The boats keep sailing and the brokers keep paying. Every drowned son's family learns the state shrugged.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: The reckoning you postponed has matured into a crisis. The jails are way too full, mostly with your enemies who never got a fair trial. People are getting sick.

left.label: Let the people who haven't had a trial go home for now.
left.tooltip: A prison of the un-charged is a scandal.
left.reveal: Thousands walk free pending trial; the jails decompress. Your hardliners call it surrender to the opposition.

right.label: Keep them all in jail and try to build more rooms.
right.tooltip: The streets are safer with them inside.
right.reveal: You keep the jails full and pour money into more bars. The outbreak spreads, and the world counts your political prisoners.

speaker: Fire Service Director
prompt: A new chapter, the same antagonists, less room to manoeuvre. A secret chemical room caught fire and many people died. The fire boss wants to shut down every unsafe building, but your rich friends own them.

left.label: Shut down all the unsafe buildings.
left.tooltip: No more pyres in the old city.
left.reveal: Inspectors padlock hundreds of illegal premises. Commerce screams and powerful landlords mobilise against the crackdown.

right.label: Just give them a small fine and let them stay open.
right.tooltip: You cannot empty half the old city.
right.reveal: A few fines, a press conference, and the lanes reopen as before. The next fire is already being stacked, drum by drum.

speaker: Immigration Director
prompt: The old fault line widens; this time there is no quiet option. Getting a passport takes forever unless you pay a secret bribe. You can make a new computer system to stop the bribes, but your workers will be mad.

left.label: Make the new computer system to stop the bribes.
left.tooltip: A passport is a right, not a bribe.
left.reveal: The brokers lose their grip and applicants their agony. The displaced officers sabotage and slander the new system.

right.label: Leave it alone and let the workers keep taking bribes.
right.tooltip: Do not provoke the whole department at once.
right.reveal: The counters stay, and so does the toll on every citizen. The racket hums on under your seal.

speaker: VIP Protocol Officer
prompt: Time has not healed it — the conflict comes back armed. A very important leader's fast car hit a poor man. The leader didn't stop, and an ambulance was stuck behind them. Everyone saw the video.

left.label: Punish the important leader and stop letting them drive so fast.
left.tooltip: No siren outruns an ambulance.
left.reveal: You publicly clip the privilege and the minister. The cabinet's grandees fume at the humiliation; the street cheers.

right.label: Protect the leader and try to hide the story.
right.tooltip: One does not feed a colleague to a mob.
right.reveal: The story is smothered and the convoys roll on. The dead man's family, and the watching city, file it away.

speaker: NID Database Chief
prompt: Once again, and with the whole house holding its breath. A computer expert wants to connect everyone's fingerprints and photos to cameras that watch the city. This could find bad guys, but it also spies on everyone.

left.label: Connect the cameras to spy on everyone.
left.tooltip: A state that sees all fears nothing.
left.reveal: You gain a near-omniscient eye over the country. When its reach leaks, the outrage at home and abroad is volcanic.

right.label: Make strict rules so the cameras can't spy on innocent people.
right.tooltip: A tool that watches everyone is a chain on everyone.
right.reveal: You keep the ID system civil, not panoptic. You lose an instrument of control and keep a shred of liberty.

speaker: Cyber Crime Unit Head
prompt: The matter resurfaces in a harder year, patience exhausted. Bad gangs online are stealing people's money using games. Some of your own young helpers are secretly part of the gangs.

left.label: Stop everyone, even your own helpers.
left.tooltip: Cut the scam off at the local hand.
left.reveal: Arrests roll up the fraud rings and the party-linked agents both. A slice of your youth wing is suddenly facing court.

right.label: Only go after the gangs from other countries, not your helpers.
right.tooltip: Do not jail our own boys over a side hustle.
right.reveal: You block sites and spare the agents. The fraud reroutes and the public keeps bleeding small sums into the dark.

speaker: Police Recruitment Board
prompt: A later season of rule, the stakes now openly existential. People have to pay giant bribes just to get a job as a police officer. This makes them bad police officers later.

left.label: Stop the bribes and only hire the best people.
left.tooltip: A bought constable polices for his broker.
left.reveal: Honest recruits enter a cleaner force. The recruitment mafias and the local bosses who ran them lose a fortune and turn hostile.

right.label: Let people keep paying bribes for jobs.
right.tooltip: Do not war with every thana at once.
right.reveal: The jobs keep selling and the new constables keep extorting to clear their debts. The rot renews itself.

speaker: Transport Authority Chief
prompt: It comes around again, and the margin for error is gone. The city is full of old, broken, dangerous buses run by mean groups. If you get rid of the bad buses, the groups might strike and stop all traffic.

left.label: Get rid of the broken, dangerous buses.
left.tooltip: A city cannot run on rolling coffins.
left.reveal: Dangerous buses come off the road and the air clears a little. The transport syndicates shut the city down in retaliation.

right.label: Be nice to the mean groups so they don't strike.
right.tooltip: They can paralyse the capital in an hour.
right.reveal: You trade enforcement for peace with the bosses. The coffins keep rolling and the gridlock thickens.

speaker: Counter-Terror Chief
prompt: The same demand, but the reign is older and the knives sharper. After a scary attack, the police want to break into homes to stop bad guys before they do anything. This might make innocent people angry.

left.label: Let the police break into homes.
left.tooltip: Strike the cells before they strike us.
left.reveal: The plots are disrupted and the world credits your resolve. Heavy-handed raids on the wrong doors breed quiet new grievances.

right.label: Be careful and try to teach people not to be bad guys instead.
right.tooltip: You cannot raid a sermon out of a mind.
right.reveal: You blend precise arrests with community work. Slower reassurance for the West, but a thinner recruiting ground over time.

speaker: Slum Eviction Magistrate
prompt: Years deep, the unresolved returns to be resolved for good. A rich builder wants to tear down a giant neighborhood where very poor people live so he can build something fancy.

left.label: Tear down the poor people's homes for the builder.
left.tooltip: The land is worth more than the shanties.
left.reveal: The towers will rise and the treasury will profit. Half a million are scattered into the monsoon, and they remember your name.

right.label: Stop the builder and let the poor people stay safely.
right.tooltip: You do not bulldoze your own people for a tower.
right.reveal: You give the slum legal footing and basic services. The developer and his patrons lose a fortune and your favour.

speaker: Special Branch Analyst
prompt: The conflict reignites, and the world is watching this time. Your spies have a list of writers and complainers. They want to follow them and scare them so they stop complaining.

left.label: Let the spies follow and scare them.
left.tooltip: Know your critics before they move.
left.reveal: You blind-side dissent before it organises. When the watchlist leaks, every name becomes a martyr and a headline.

right.label: Tell the spies to throw away the list.
right.tooltip: A state afraid of its scholars is already weak.
right.reveal: You stand down the dragnet. You lose an early-warning net and keep the country a place thought can still be spoken.

speaker: Border Guard Sector Commander
prompt: A familiar crisis, escalated by everything you let slide. People sneak things over the border to make money, and guards shoot them. You could build safe, legal markets there instead.

left.label: Build safe, legal markets on the border.
left.tooltip: Trade in daylight beats trade under fire.
left.reveal: Sanctioned haats pull commerce into the open and cut the killings. The smuggling syndicates lose their margins and snarl.

right.label: Keep it illegal and punish the sneekers.
right.tooltip: Smuggling is smuggling; stamp it out.
right.reveal: Raids choke the trade and the border economy collapses. The villages, robbed of their living, curse the state that 'saved' them.

speaker: Riot Control Commander
prompt: The fault line cracks wider; the decision can no longer wait. A giant crowd of your enemies is having a peaceful meeting. Your police boss wants to use sticks and gas to make them run away.

left.label: Use sticks and gas to make them run away.
left.tooltip: A crowd that size is a threat, not a meeting.
left.reveal: The avenue is cleared in a haze of gas and blood. The footage hardens the opposition and shames you abroad.

right.label: Let them have their peaceful meeting.
right.tooltip: Let them shout; tomorrow they go home.
right.reveal: The rally swells, peaks, and disperses on its own. Your hawks call it weakness; the day passes without a martyr.

speaker: Disappearances Liaison
prompt: Late in the reign, the old question demands a heavier answer. Mothers of people who were kidnapped by the police are standing together crying. The world wants to send a team to investigate.

left.label: Let the world team come and find the truth.
left.tooltip: A state that hides its vans has lost its soul.
left.reveal: Some of the missing surface; the world cautiously applauds the candour. The agencies responsible feel the ground shift beneath them.

right.label: Keep the team out and force the mothers to leave.
right.tooltip: No foreigner audits our security.
right.reveal: You bar the investigators and clear the vigil. The mothers' grief becomes the opposition's sharpest, most enduring weapon.

speaker: Domestic Worker Advocate
prompt: It returns once more — and this round may be the one remembered. A little girl working as a maid in a rich house was hurt very badly. A helper wants to check all rich houses to make sure maids are safe.

left.label: Check the houses to keep the maids safe.
left.tooltip: No child should vanish behind a rich gate.
left.reveal: Inspections begin to pierce the private cruelty. The affluent — many of them your supporters — resent the intrusion.

right.label: Say no because it is their private house.
right.tooltip: The state does not enter the drawing room.
right.reveal: You leave the gates closed and the maids unseen. The next tortured child is another headline you will have to manage.

speaker: Election Security Chief
prompt: The reckoning circles back, grown teeth in the waiting. Voting day is coming and people might fight. You can put the army everywhere to keep it fair, or only put them where it helps you win.

left.label: Put the army everywhere to keep the vote completely fair.
left.tooltip: Let the count be clean or not at all.
left.reveal: The vote passes with rare calm and credibility. Your own machine grumbles that you gave away the home advantage.

right.label: Put the army only where it helps you win.
right.tooltip: Security is also strategy.
right.reveal: Your centres are safe; the opposition's are not. You bank the seats and the observers' damning footnotes both.

speaker: Acid & Dowry Cell
prompt: Another term, the same antagonist, the stakes raised again. Mean people are throwing bad chemicals that burn women's faces. You can make it very hard to buy these chemicals, but store owners will complain.

left.label: Make it very hard to buy the bad chemicals and punish the mean people.
left.tooltip: A face burned for a dowry is a state's failure.
left.reveal: Open acid vanishes from the bazaars and convictions quicken. The chemical traders lobby and grumble, but the wards see fewer victims.

right.label: Let the stores keep selling the chemicals easily.
right.tooltip: Do not strangle a whole trade over a few cases.
right.reveal: Acid stays cheap and available. Another disfigured woman, another vigil, another file you will answer for.

speaker: Crowd Safety Officer
prompt: The matter, long deferred, lands with the force of a verdict. Two huge events are happening at the same time. Last year, a crowd pushed too hard and people died. A helper wants strict rules to keep crowds small and safe.

left.label: Make strict rules to keep the crowds safe.
left.tooltip: I will not preside over another stampede.
left.reveal: The events run safely under tight management. Some call the controls heavy-handed and the charity less generous.

right.label: Let everyone go without rules.
right.tooltip: Do not fence in the people's gatherings.
right.reveal: The crowds flow free and vast. The risk you waved away is one bad surge from becoming your next tragedy.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: Years on and unhealed, the conflict returns to claim its answer. Bad guys with weapons took over a fancy restaurant and are hurting people inside.

left.label: Send the police rushing in immediately to fight them.
left.tooltip: Every hour of delay is another execution.
left.reveal: Commandos breach and end the siege in blood. Some hostages die in the assault, but the militants are wiped out and the state shows it will not be held to ransom.

right.label: Wait and try to talk to them to save lives safely.
right.tooltip: A panicked assault fills the morgue.
right.reveal: You hold off and work for a safer resolution. The standoff drags agonisingly and the watching world frets, but a patient rescue may yet save those still alive inside.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The same fault line, the harder hour, the narrower path. The bad guys from the restaurant turned out to be rich, smart kids from good families. Your friends are terrified.

left.label: Have the police arrest lots of young people to be safe.
left.tooltip: Root out the rot before it spreads.
left.reveal: Mass detentions sweep up the young and the bearded. A few cells are broken, but the dragnet radicalises as many as it catches, and the abuses pile up.

right.label: Try to gently teach young people not to join bad groups.
right.tooltip: You cannot arrest your way out of an idea.
right.reveal: You build counselling, monitoring, and community programmes instead of mass arrests. The results are slow and invisible, but the pipeline of recruits begins, quietly, to thin.

speaker: Inspector-General of Police
prompt: It comes back sharpened by every season you survived. A fake story about strangers stealing kids for a bridge is making mobs beat innocent people to death.

left.label: Tell police they can shoot the angry mobs.
left.tooltip: A mob that kills must fear the gun.
left.reveal: Police open fire to break the worst mobs and the lynchings ebb. The deaths at police hands draw their own outcry, and the underlying panic still smoulders online.

right.label: Stop the fake story and arrest the people who started the mob.
right.tooltip: Kill the lie and the mob dissolves.
right.reveal: You flood the networks with truth, arrest instigators, and protect the accused. It is slower than bullets, but the panic deflates without the state adding to the body count.

speaker: Home Secretary
prompt: A later year of power, the old crisis now a true test of it. A huge group of religious people blocked the whole city. They demand you destroy a statue they don't like.

left.label: Send the police at night to force them all to leave.
left.tooltip: The capital cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: Police and paramilitaries clear the square in a dark, bloody operation. The occupation ends and rumours of a massacre spread; the movement gains martyrs and a grievance that will not die.

right.label: Talk to them and maybe give them a little bit of what they want.
right.tooltip: A square emptied by talk leaves no martyrs.
right.reveal: You move the disputed sculpture and promise to 'study' their demands, and the occupation disperses. Secularists feel betrayed by the concession, but the capital is spared a massacre.

speaker: Intelligence Procurement Chief
prompt: The unresolved returns, and this time it will not be deferred. You can buy a super-secret tool to read everyone's phone messages. But if you buy it, everyone will eventually find out you spy on them.

left.label: Buy the super-secret spy tool.
left.tooltip: A leader who cannot read the room is blind.
left.reveal: You acquire the spyware and the secrets pour in. Every rival's phone is an open book — until the purchase leaks, and the word 'surveillance state' attaches to your name for good.

right.label: Do not buy the spy tool.
right.tooltip: A tool that reads everyone eventually reads its master's enemies into power.
right.reveal: You pass on the spyware. You forgo a formidable surveillance edge, but you are spared the day an exposé reveals you bugged judges, journalists, and your own ministers.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: A term later, the same fault line tears open again. The jails are completely packed because you arrested so many people who don't like you. You can let the harmless ones go home.

left.label: Keep them all in jail and try to build more space.
left.tooltip: A freed activist is a refilled street.
left.reveal: You keep the jails packed and order new prisons built. The opposition stays caged and the streets quiet, but the deaths in custody and the cost of the cells both keep climbing.

right.label: Let the harmless people go home.
right.tooltip: A jail bursting with the innocent shames the state.
right.reveal: You free thousands held on flimsy mass cases. The hardliners warn the streets will fill, but the custodial deaths fall and the courts and jails can breathe.

speaker: University Affairs Officer
prompt: Years into your rule, the matter you thought buried resurfaces — harder. Students from your own team beat another student until he died just for writing something bad about you online.

left.label: Protect your team members so they don't go to jail.
left.tooltip: I do not feed my own loyalists to a mob.
left.reveal: You quietly protect the killers and float counter-narratives. The cover-up enrages the campuses further, and the dead student becomes a permanent symbol of your wing's impunity.

right.label: Send them to jail for what they did.
right.tooltip: A boy tortured to death is not a loyalty test.
right.reveal: You let the cadres be arrested and charged. The student wing feels betrayed and the machine grumbles, but the campuses, stunned, see a flicker of accountability.

speaker: Smart City Director
prompt: A new season of power, an old problem with sharper teeth. You can put cameras everywhere that know exactly who everyone is and where they go. It stops crime but means no one has any privacy.

left.label: Put up the cameras that track everyone's faces.
left.tooltip: A watched city is a safe city.
left.reveal: Cameras and face-tracking blanket the capital. Street crime and terror plots get easier to foil, and so does following every activist from home to rally to home.

right.label: Put up regular cameras that don't track faces.
right.tooltip: I will deter the mugger without indexing the marcher.
right.reveal: You install the cameras but bar the face-recognition layer. You gain real crime deterrence without building a tool to map every dissident's movements.

speaker: Security Reform Adviser
prompt: The crisis returns, and this time the cabinet is watching. Your strict police stopped killing bad guys because the world was watching. But now crime is coming back. Your strict friends want the police to start killing again.

left.label: Let the police start secretly killing again.
left.tooltip: Fear was the only law the criminals respected.
left.reveal: You ease the leash and the 'crossfire' deaths quietly return. Crime dips and the hardliners cheer, but the sanctions harden and the reform's brief promise dies.

right.label: Stop them from killing and make them do things by the rules.
right.tooltip: A force that murders cannot be trusted to police.
right.reveal: You lock in the restraint and rebuild the battalion around oversight and proper investigation. Crime-fighting is slower and the hardliners scornful, but the sanctions ease and a killing machine becomes a police force.

speaker: Police Reform Commissioner
prompt: Once more the issue lands on your desk, the stakes higher than before. Local police stations are very mean and won't help anyone unless they pay a bribe. You can try to make them clean and nice, but the police will fight you.

left.label: Force the police stations to be clean and nice.
left.tooltip: A police that sells justice is a syndicate in uniform.
left.reveal: You impose digital records, citizen oversight, and anti-bribery teams. The rank-and-file seethe and slow-walk, but ordinary people start getting served without paying for it.

right.label: Leave the mean police stations alone.
right.tooltip: A demoralised force is a useless one.
right.reveal: You decline to disturb the station-house economy. The constables stay content and pliable, and the poor keep paying a bribe for every scrap of justice the state owes them free.

speaker: Anti-Trafficking Chief
prompt: Another year survived, and the reckoning circles back. Bad guys are putting poor men on broken boats to go to another country, and the police are helping the bad guys.

left.label: Stop everyone, even the police who are helping.
left.tooltip: A trade in drowning men cannot have a uniform's blessing.
left.reveal: You break the syndicate and expose the officers shielding it. The trafficking route is disrupted and the corrupt purged, to international relief and some internal fury.

right.label: Only stop the boat drivers and leave the police alone.
right.tooltip: Some uniforms are better left unexamined.
right.reveal: You arrest the front-line smugglers and leave their protectors untouched. The route reopens under new management within weeks, and the boats keep sailing into the dark.

speaker: Religious Harmony Officer
prompt: The ground has shifted; the same demand arrives with new menace. A big angry crowd is attacking a small group of people who pray differently.

left.label: Use the police to protect the small group.
left.tooltip: The state shields the few from the many, or it shields no one.
left.reveal: You deploy security to defend the sect's mosques and arrest the instigators. The hardline preachers turn their fury on you, but the constitution's promise of equal protection holds.

right.label: Give the angry crowd what they want to make them happy.
right.tooltip: I will not burn the country for one small sect.
right.reveal: You quietly distance the state from the sect to placate the mob. The hardliners are triumphant, the violence subsides, and a small community learns the state will trade them for peace.

speaker: Cultural Affairs Officer
prompt: History rhymes: the conflict reopens deeper than last time. People want to have a big, fun new year party with music and masks. But grumpy people say it is bad and threaten to ruin it with a bomb.

left.label: Throw the party anyway and put lots of guards around it.
left.tooltip: Our culture will not be cancelled by a threat.
left.reveal: You ring the festival with security and it goes ahead, defiant and joyous. The cost and the tension are high, but the secular tradition stands and the militants are denied their veto.

right.label: Cancel the party to be safe and make the grumpy people happy.
right.tooltip: A dead crowd is too high a price for a parade.
right.reveal: You call it off, citing the threat. The zealots quietly celebrate the cancellation they wanted, the secularists mourn a tradition surrendered, and the militants learn that a threat is enough.

speaker: Internal Affairs Director
prompt: Mid-reign, the old wound is torn afresh. An honest police officer shared secret papers proving his boss ordered the police to kill people.

left.label: Punish the honest officer for sharing secrets and hide the papers.
left.tooltip: A leaker is a traitor whatever he reveals.
left.reveal: You jail the whistleblower and quash the story. The killings stay covered, but the lesson lands in every honest officer: speak, and the state will break you, not the killer.

right.label: Protect the honest officer and punish his bad boss.
right.tooltip: An officer with a conscience is worth ten without.
right.reveal: You shield the whistleblower and move against the commander. The old guard is shaken and warns of low morale, but a culture of impunity takes its first real blow.

speaker: District Magistrate
prompt: The pressure you deferred has come due, larger now. Your enemies are planning a giant meeting. You can use an old rule to make it illegal, but that might make them so angry they fight you.

left.label: Make the meeting illegal.
left.tooltip: A rally forbidden is a rally that cannot grow.
left.reveal: You blanket the city in an assembly ban and flood it with police. The rally is throttled, but the heavy-handed lockdown of the capital becomes the story, and the grievance hardens.

right.label: Let them have the meeting, but with safety rules.
right.tooltip: A banned gathering becomes a battle; a permitted one becomes a speech.
right.reveal: You allow the rally within agreed limits. The opposition gets its day and its crowd, but the feared confrontation never comes, and the state looks confident rather than afraid.

speaker: Arms Licensing Officer
prompt: A later term, and the quarrel has hardened into a test. Your local friends want you to give them licenses to carry guns. They say it's for safety, but they really want to scare people during elections.

left.label: Give your friends the gun licenses.
left.tooltip: An armed friend is a loyal friend.
left.reveal: You sign off on the licences and the local strongmen are armed. They deliver elections and intimidate rivals, and the country grows a little more lawless, district by district.

right.label: Say no and take guns away from them.
right.tooltip: A party with guns is a militia in waiting.
right.reveal: You clamp down on private firearms, your own side included. The local leaders feel disarmed and betrayed, but the cycle of armed political muscle loosens its grip.

speaker: Counter-Terror Commander
prompt: The country has changed, but this fault line has not — only worsened. Some people who left to join a bad terrorist group want to come back home with their kids.

left.label: Do not let them come back, or lock them in jail forever.
left.tooltip: A trained jihadi is a threat, not a project.
left.reveal: You refuse entry or jail those who slip back. The hard line plays well, but the stranded radicalise further abroad and the children pay for their fathers' choices, drawing rights criticism.

right.label: Let them come back, but watch them very closely and help them change.
right.tooltip: A child of the caliphate is still a child.
right.reveal: You bring them home into monitoring and de-radicalisation, prosecuting the guilty. It is a risk and the public is uneasy, but you reclaim the salvageable and the innocent rather than abandoning them.

speaker: Information Security Officer
prompt: It returns, as these things do, with the patience gone. You can secretly take away the special passes from reporters who write mean things about you, so they can't do their jobs.

left.label: Take away the passes from the mean reporters.
left.tooltip: Access is a privilege for the friendly.
left.reveal: The critical reporters lose their passes and their scoops. The press corps gets the message and softens, and 'managed media' becomes one more charge in the indictment of your rule.

right.label: Let all reporters keep their passes.
right.tooltip: A press corps of flatterers tells me nothing.
right.reveal: You leave access untouched, critics and all. The tough questions keep coming at every briefing, but your government keeps a free press it can point to abroad.

speaker: Prisons Inspector-General
prompt: Years on, the matter is no longer private; the press is already circling. Very bad terrorists escaped from a super safe prison because someone inside helped them.

left.label: Send police everywhere to search every corner of the country.
left.tooltip: Flood the country until they surface.
left.reveal: Checkpoints and sweeps blanket the nation. The disruption is enormous and the wrongful detentions many, and the fugitives may still slip the clumsy net.

right.label: Use smart clues to track them quietly.
right.tooltip: A scalpel catches what a sledgehammer scatters.
right.reveal: You hunt the escapees quietly through intelligence and informants. It is slower and the public nervous, but the country is not paralysed and the inside helpers are traced too.

speaker: Industrial Police Chief
prompt: The same knot, retied tighter, lands before you once more. Thousands of factory workers blocked the road because they haven't been paid. The factory owners want you to send the police to beat them and clear the road.

left.label: Send the police to beat the workers and clear the road.
left.tooltip: The export zones cannot be held hostage.
left.reveal: The police clear the highways with batons and tear gas, and a worker or two die. The owners are grateful and the lines restart, but the deaths become a global stain on the garment brand.

right.label: Help the workers get the money they are owed.
right.tooltip: Unpaid workers are a fire; do not fan it with batons.
right.reveal: You force the owners to pay the arrears and mediate a raise. The donors grumble at the squeeze, but the highways clear peacefully and the workers go back with their dignity.

speaker: Land Records Commissioner
prompt: Another monsoon, another return of an argument you cannot end. Powerful people in your group stole land from a smaller religion using a bad, old rule. You can force them to give it back.

left.label: Force them to give the stolen land back.
left.tooltip: Land stolen under a bigot's law must go home.
left.reveal: You push to return the seized property to its rightful owners. The connected grabbers rage and resist parcel by parcel, but a historic injustice begins, at last, to be undone.

right.label: Let them keep the stolen land.
right.tooltip: I will not evict my own base for old ghosts.
right.reveal: You let the grabbers keep their spoils. Your local power-holders are secured, and the minority families learn once more that the law that robbed them will not give it back.

speaker: NID Database Director
prompt: Deep into the reign, the issue demands a final-sounding answer. You can connect everyone's ID cards, phones, and bank accounts into one giant computer file. It is helpful, but lets the government know everything about everyone.

left.label: Build the giant computer file.
left.tooltip: A state that knows its citizens governs them well.
